skill.name: mcp-prompts-guide
skill.description: Create MCP prompt templates for rmcp servers with arguments, context injection, examples, and tests.
skill.summary: Guide developers through MCP prompt design and Rust rmcp prompt implementation.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found they are false positives in Markdown and Rust prompt examples. No shell execution, network access, credential access, malware intent, or prompt injection attempt was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive External Command Detections
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur at Rust Markdown fence boundaries and prompt-building examples. The skill is documentation guidance and does not instruct the assistant to run shell commands or execute external programs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown code fences around Rust examples or non-executable prompt construction. No std::process, shell string, spawn call, or command invocation appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detections
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are keyword collisions in prose, field names, and Rust examples. Review found no hashing implementation, no cryptographic API use, and no recommendation to use MD5, SHA-1, or another weak algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains prompt design text and Rust string formatting, not cryptographic operations. The duplicate static hit at line 151 maps to a prompt description string, so it does not add independent risk.

content.value_statement: MCP prompt design can be hard to structure consistently across tools and tasks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users through reusable rmcp prompt templates, arguments, context, examples, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP prompt concepts and prompt discovery patterns.
Shows Rust rmcp examples for listing prompts and returning prompt responses.
Demonstrates required and optional prompt arguments with defaults.
Covers code review, API design, learning, debugging, and refactoring prompt patterns.
Provides prompt testing patterns for unit and integration tests.
Describes dynamic context injection from documentation, examples, and best practices.
limitations: Provides guidance and examples, not a complete runnable MCP server.
Focuses on Rust rmcp patterns rather than every MCP SDK.
Does not validate prompt quality against live model responses.
Requires developers to adapt examples to their own types and error handling.

use_cases.title: Add prompts to an MCP server
use_cases.description: Use the examples to design prompt names, descriptions, arguments, and response messages for a Rust rmcp service.

use_cases.title: Standardize team prompt templates
use_cases.description: Create reusable prompts for code review, API design, debugging, refactoring, and learning workflows.

use_cases.title: Plan prompt tests before release
use_cases.description: Use the testing sections to check required arguments, prompt lookup, response structure, and end-to-end behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic MCP prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a basic MCP prompt for an rmcp server. The prompt should be named [name], describe [task], and require [arguments].

prompt_templates.title: Design prompt arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this prompt goal and propose required arguments, optional arguments, defaults, and validation rules: [goal].

prompt_templates.title: Add context injection
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an MCP prompt that injects project context, documentation, examples, and best practices for this workflow: [workflow].

prompt_templates.title: Audit a prompt system
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my MCP prompt implementation for missing arguments, weak structure, unsafe assumptions, test gaps, and maintainability issues.

output_examples.input: A developer wants a code review prompt with language and focus arguments.
output_examples.output: The response outlines a prompt name, a clear description, one required language argument, one optional focus argument, and user-facing review instructions.

output_examples.input: A team needs a debugging prompt that starts a multi-turn session.
output_examples.output: The response proposes a system message, an assistant opening question, expected user details, and checks for error messages and relevant code.

output_examples.input: A project needs context-aware prompts backed by documentation.
output_examples.output: The response describes loading relevant documentation, examples, and best practices before composing a structured user message.

best_practices: Define clear prompt names, descriptions, and argument requirements before writing implementation code.
Validate required arguments and provide explicit defaults for optional arguments.
Test prompt listing, prompt retrieval, missing arguments, and response structure.
anti_patterns: Do not create vague prompts that lack a target task or expected output.
Do not inject large context blocks without relevance checks or structure.
Do not rely on examples without testing required and optional argument combinations.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps developers design MCP prompt templates for Rust rmcp servers.

faq.question: Does it include runnable server code?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that developers adapt to their server.

faq.question: Which tools can use the guidance?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it help with prompt arguments?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers required arguments, optional arguments, defaults, and validation.

faq.question: Does it cover testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes unit and integration test patterns for prompt listing and retrieval.

faq.question: Does it perform external actions?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance and does not execute commands or access networks.
